question: What are current approaches to evaluating tool use in LLM agents?

claim_lines: Current approaches to evaluating tool use in LLM agents cluster into three layers: (1) survey-level taxonomies that separate **what** to evaluate from **how** to evaluate, (2) benchmarks that stress **stateful, multi-turn, interactive** tool execution, and (3) framework/provider workflows that use **traces, graders, human review, and LLM-as-judge** scoring. The main practical shift is away from output-only scoring and toward trajectory-aware evaluation of the agent’s decisions and interactions. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.21504))

- **Task success** — Measured as end-to-end completion or milestone attainment in realistic workflows, not just final text quality. Surveys and benchmark docs emphasize behavior/capability outcomes plus intermediate/final milestones. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.21504))

- **Tool-call correctness** — Checked by trace grading and trajectory inspection: did the agent choose the right tool and execute the intended workflow? ([platform.openai.com](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/agent-evals))

- **Argument validity** — Commonly handled with heuristics, human annotation, and LLM-as-judge scoring of reasoning/justification quality, often calibrated against human feedback. ([langchain.com](https://www.langchain.com/evaluation))

- **Recovery from tool errors** — Stateful benchmarks like ToolSandbox are designed for implicit state dependencies and dynamic evaluation, which better exposes failure recovery and robustness over long trajectories. ([machinelearning.apple.com](https://machinelearning.apple.com/research/toolsandbox-stateful-conversational-llm-benchmark))

- **Cost and latency** — Current practice treats these as operational evaluation concerns: offline/online eval loops, regression checks, and tracing systems are used to control quality without adding unacceptable runtime overhead. ([langchain.com](https://www.langchain.com/evaluation))
